skill.name: aws-serverless
skill.description: Specialized skill for building production-ready serverless applications on AWS. Covers Lambda functions, API Gateway, DynamoDB, SQS/SNS event-driven patterns, SAM/CDK deployment, and cold start optimizations.
skill.summary: Build production-ready AWS serverless applications with Lambda, API Gateway, and event-driven patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill provides documentation and code patterns for AWS serverless development. Static analyzer flagged 34 potential issues, but all are false positives triggered by YAML code blocks and standard AWS terminology. The skill contains only legitimate code examples for Lambda handlers, SAM templates, and infrastructure configuration. No malicious patterns found.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Shell Command Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer detected backtick patterns (`) in YAML code fences. These are markdown code block delimiters, not shell execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The backtick character is used in markdown to start YAML code blocks (```yaml), not for Ruby/shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Windows SAM Database
dangerous_patterns.description: Analyzer matched 'SAM' acronym to Windows Security Account Manager database. In this context, SAM refers to AWS Serverless Application Model.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill is about AWS serverless development. SAM stands for AWS Serverless Application Model, a legitimate AWS infrastructure-as-code framework.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL
dangerous_patterns.description: Analyzer flagged AWS API Gateway URL as suspicious external network call.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL 'https://${HttpApi}.execute-api.${AWS::Region}.amazonaws.com/prod' is a standard AWS CloudFormation output for API Gateway, not a malicious external endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: Analyzer flagged relative import path as path traversal vulnerability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: '../lib/dynamodb' is a standard Node.js relative require() path for importing local modules, not a path traversal attack.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Environment Variable Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Analyzer flagged AWS Lambda environment variable patterns as sensitive data access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: process.env.TABLE_NAME and os.environ['TABLE_NAME'] are standard AWS Lambda patterns for accessing configuration via environment variables. This is the recommended practice.

content.value_statement: This skill helps developers create production-ready AWS serverless applications with proper Lambda patterns, API Gateway integration, and event-driven architectures.
actual_capabilities: Generate proper Lambda handler code in Node.js and Python with error handling
Create AWS SAM templates for API Gateway and Lambda integration
Implement SQS event-driven processing patterns with retry and DLQ support
Show best practices for cold start optimization and VPC configuration
Provide anti-patterns and sharp edges guidance for serverless development
limitations: Does not provision actual AWS resources or credentials
Does not execute deployed functions or make AWS API calls
Does not include CDK infrastructure code patterns
Does not cover Step Functions or AppSync GraphQL APIs

use_cases.title: New Serverless Project Setup
use_cases.description: Generate proper Lambda handler structure with DynamoDB integration, error handling, and API Gateway response format.
use_cases.target_user: Developers new to AWS serverless who need starting templates

use_cases.title: Event-Driven Architecture
use_cases.description: Create SQS-triggered Lambda functions with batch processing, partial failure handling, and dead letter queue configuration.
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineers building async processing pipelines

use_cases.title: Infrastructure as Code
use_cases.description: Write SAM templates with proper IAM policies, environment variables, CORS settings, and resource definitions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Lambda Handler
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Node.js Lambda handler function that reads from DynamoDB using the AWS SDK v3. Include proper error handling and return an API Gateway compatible response.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new serverless API endpoint

prompt_templates.title: API Gateway Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a SAM template that creates an HTTP API with Lambda integration. Include GET and POST endpoints with DynamoDB read and write policies.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building REST API with Lambda backend

prompt_templates.title: SQS Queue Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Lambda function that processes SQS messages in batches. Include partial failure handling that reports failed items back to SQS for retry.

prompt_templates.title: Cold Start Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Show best practices for reducing Lambda cold start times including memory allocation, dependency management, and VPC considerations.

output_examples.input: Create a Lambda handler that reads from DynamoDB
output_examples.output: A Node.js Lambda handler with AWS SDK v3, proper error handling, DynamoDBDocumentClient setup, and API Gateway response format with CORS headers.

output_examples.input: Write SAM template for HTTP API with Lambda
output_examples.output: A SAM template showing HttpApi definition, Lambda function with DynamoDBReadPolicy and DynamoDBCrudPolicy, and proper CORS configuration.

output_examples.input: Implement SQS batch processing with retry logic
output_examples.output: Lambda handler that iterates through SQS records, catches individual failures, tracks batchItemFailures, and returns them for SQS retry mechanism.

best_practices: Initialize AWS SDK clients and database connections outside the Lambda handler for reuse across invocations
Use DynamoDBDocumentClient for simplified attribute marshaling and unmarshaling
Configure appropriate timeout values matching your function execution time plus overhead
Use provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive applications
anti_patterns: Do not create monolithic Lambda functions that handle multiple unrelated operations
Avoid including large dependencies that are rarely used to reduce deployment package size
Do not make synchronous calls inside VPC-attached Lambda without considering ENI attachment overhead
Avoid using wildcard CORS AllowOrigins in production unless explicitly required

faq.question: What runtime does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill provides patterns for Node.js and Python Lambda runtimes.

faq.question: Does this skill create actual AWS resources?
faq.answer: No, this skill generates code templates and patterns. You must deploy them using SAM CLI or CDK.

faq.question: How do I handle DynamoDB connections in Lambda?
faq.answer: Create the DynamoDB client outside the handler function so it is reused across invocations.

faq.question: What is the recommended batch size for SQS Lambda?
faq.answer: Start with batch size 10 and tune based on your message processing time and failure rate.

faq.question: How do I reduce Lambda cold start times?
faq.answer: Increase memory allocation (increases CPU), minimize dependencies, use provisioned concurrency, and avoid VPC if not needed.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for VPC-attached Lambda functions?
faq.answer: Yes, but be aware of ENI attachment overhead that affects cold starts. Consider using VPC endpoints for DynamoDB.
